questions.text: In some reactions, a product formed acts as a catalyst. This phenomenon is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Negative catalysts are called inhibitors, they inhibit the reactions.
In heterogeneous catalysis, the catalyst is in different phase from the reactants.
Activation of a catalyst refers to activating it to perform task.

If product formed act as a catalyst is called autocatalyst and reaction is called auto catalysis.In autocatalysis, the reaction is catalyzed by one of its products and that catalyst is called Autocatalyst. One of the simplest examples of this is in the oxidation of a solution of oxalic acid by an acidified solution of potassium manganate (VII) (potassium permanganate).The reaction is very slow at room temperature.

questions.explanation: If product formed act as a catalyst is called autocatalyst and reaction is called auto catalysis.One of the simplest examples of this is in the oxidation of a solution of oxalic acid by an acidified solution of potassium manganate (VII) (potassium permanganate).The reaction is very slow at room temperature.

questions.text: The reaction rate in forward direction decreases with the passage of time because
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reaction rate in forward direction decreases with the passage of time because the concentration of reactants decreases. As the reaction proceeds, the reactants are consumed and their concentration decreases. This means that there are fewer reactant molecules available to collide and react, which slows down the reaction rate.

Concentration of product decreases is not correct because the concentration of products does not have a direct effect on the reaction rate in the forward direction. The concentration of products can only affect the reaction rate if it is high enough to inhibit the reaction.

The order of reaction changes is not correct because the order of reaction only determines how the reaction rate depends on the concentration of the reactants. The order of reaction does not have a direct effect on the reaction rate in the forward direction.

Temperature of the system changes is not correct because the temperature of the system affects the rate of collisions between the reactant molecules. However, the temperature of the system does not have a direct effect on the reaction rate in the forward direction. The reaction rate in the forward direction is determined by the concentration of the reactants and the order of reaction.

questions.explanation: The reaction rate in forward direction decreases with the passage of time because the concentration of reactants decreases. As the reaction proceeds, the reactants are consumed and their concentration decreases. This means that there are fewer reactant molecules available to collide and react, which slows down the reaction rate.

questions.text: If energy of activated complex is close to energy of reactants, it means that the reaction is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is correct because if the energy of the activated complex is close to the energy of the reactants, it indicates that the reactants can easily reach the activated state, facilitating a quick conversion to products.
This option suggests a speed that is neither fast nor slow, which does not accurately reflect the relationship described in the question where the energy levels are closely aligned.
This option implies a significant barrier to reaction, which contradicts the scenario where the energy of the activated complex is close to that of the reactants, enabling rapid reaction.
This option indicates an even greater barrier to reaction. As with the previous options, it does not align with the premise of low energy differential between reactants and the activated complex.

questions.explanation: If energy of activated complex is close to energy of reactant, then reaction is fast, and spontaneous. Activated state is the state when reactants gain sufficient energy to be converted into product hence, if energy of reactants is already close to the energy of activated complex, so they will not take time and energy to get converted to activated state. Thus, they will react spontaneously and fast.

questions.text: According to the collision theory of bimolecular reactions in gas phase, minimum amount of energy is required for an effective collision is known as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This term refers to the overall energy change during a chemical reaction, not the minimum energy required for effective collisions.
The rate of reaction indicates how quickly a reaction occurs but does not specify the energy required for collisions between molecules.

options.text: Has no effect on the reaction
options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests that energy does not influence the reaction, while in reality, energy is critical for effective molecular collisions.
This is the correct term that describes the minimum energy required for a successful collision in bimolecular reactions, facilitating the formation of an activated complex.

questions.explanation: The minimum energy required for an effective collision in a bimolecular gas-phase reaction is called the energy of activation.

questions.text: If the energy of activation of a chemical reaction is very low, the rate of that chemical reaction is observed to be very high because?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Concentration of the reactants becomes irrelevant
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the concentration of reactants, still, is a factor regardless of other factors such as low activation energy. (Same goes for all other factors that affect the rate of reaction).

options.text: Reaction proceeds without any transition state
options.explanation: This option is incorrect, as the transition state is always present regardless of activation energy.

options.text: Number of efficient or fruitful collisions increase
options.explanation: This option is correct, as low activation energy means that many more reactant particles have the minimum energy required to produce products so, with a greater number of particles possessing energy equal to or greater than the activation energy collide, they produce products. Hence, more fruitful collisions occur.

options.text: Molecules of the reactants move slowly
options.explanation: This option is incorrect, as reactant particles move slowly if there is less heat energy applied, which was not mentioned in the question.

questions.explanation: Option A is incorrect because the concentration of reactants, still, is a factor regardless of other factors such as low activation energy. (Same goes for all other factors that affect the rate of reaction) Option B is incorrect, as the transition state is always present regardless of activation energy. Option C is correct, as low activation energy means that many more reactant particles have the minimum energy required to produce products so, with a greater number of particles possessing energy equal to or greater than the activation energy collide, they produce products. Hence, more fruitful collisions occur. Option D is incorrect, as reactant particles move slowly if there is less heat energy applied, which was not mentioned in the question.
hints: rate of that chemical reaction is observed

questions.text: The influence of temperature on reaction rate is predicted by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The free energy change (ΔG) is a thermodynamic quantity that is not directly related to the rate of reaction but rather to the spontaneity and equilibrium of a process. It does not specifically predict the influence of temperature on reaction rates.

The Van der Waals equation describes the behavior of real gases and includes factors such as particle volume and intermolecular forces. It does not relate to the rate of chemical reactions or the influence of temperature on reaction rates.

The Arrhenius equation is used to describe how temperature affects the rate of a reaction. It shows that an increase in temperature results in an increase in reaction rate, as it lowers the activation energy barrier for the reaction.
The term 'kinetic equation' is a generic term and does not specifically refer to how temperature affects reaction rates. The Arrhenius equation is the specific model used for this purpose.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the Arrhenius equation, which explicitly shows how an increase in temperature leads to an increase in reaction rate by decreasing the activation energy barrier. The other options do not specifically address the influence of temperature on the rate of reaction:Free energy change: Relates to spontaneity and equilibrium, not reaction rates.Van der Waals equation: Describes real gas behavior, not chemical reaction rates.Kinetic equation: A general term that does not specifically model the temperature effect on reaction rates.

questions.text: For which change of temperature, the rate of reaction become approximately double:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 293K is equivalent to 20°C, which does not specify a 10°C rise in temperature.
283K is equivalent to 10°C, but the question requires a temperature change, not a value.
Although 20°C is a valid temperature, the reaction rate approximately doubles with a 10°C increase, not 20°C.
Correct. Generally, the rate of a reaction approximately doubles with a 10°C increase in temperature.

questions.explanation: The rate of a reaction approximately doubles for every 10°C rise in temperature. This occurs because increasing the temperature increases the kinetic energy of the molecules, leading to more frequent and energetic collisions. Option D is correct because it specifies a 10°C change, which aligns with the rule of thumb for reaction rates. The other options either specify a temperature value rather than a change or use an incorrect temperature change.

questions.text: It is experimentally found that catalyst is used to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A positive catalyst facilitates the reaction by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy, which increases the rate of reaction without being consumed in the process.
This option is incorrect because catalysts work to lower the activation energy, not increase it. Increasing activation energy would slow down the reaction rate.
This option is not relevant to the function of a catalyst. While pH can affect reaction rates in some cases, it is not a direct effect of a catalyst.
This option is incomplete and does not specify what is being decreased. It does not relate to the role of a catalyst in reaction kinetics.

questions.explanation: A positive catalyst increases rate of reaction, decreases activation energy, decreases equilibrium time and changes mechanism of reaction.

questions.text: Role of a catalyst in a chemical reaction is to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A positive catalyst increase rate of reaction, decreases activation energy, decrease equilibrium time and changes mechanism of reaction A catalyst is a chemical substance which helps to increase the rate of the reaction. - It makes the reaction proceed faster by adjusting the activation energy of that reaction or by changing the mechanism of the reaction. - It does not get consumed by itself in the reaction.

It increases the rate of reaction by lowering activation energy. A catalyst is a chemical substance which helps to increase the rate of the reaction. - It makes the reaction proceed faster by adjusting the activation energy of that reaction or by changing the mechanism of the reaction. - It does not get consumed by itself in the reaction.
It only increases rate of reaction.

It only increases rate of reaction. A catalyst is a chemical substance which helps to increase the rate of the reaction. - It makes the reaction proceed faster by adjusting the activation energy of that reaction or by changing the mechanism of the reaction. - It does not get consumed by itself in the reaction.

questions.explanation: A positive catalyst increase rate of reaction, decreases activation energy, decrease equilibrium time and changes mechanism of reaction.

questions.text: 2A + B --> product If the reactant 'B ' is in excess, the order of reaction with respect to ' A in the given rate lawRate = k[A]2 [ B ] is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the correct option. When 'B' is in excess, the reaction behaves as a 2nd order reaction with respect to 'A'. This means that the rate of the reaction is directly proportional to the square of the concentration of 'A'.

A pseudo order reaction typically occurs when one of the reactants is in excess and its concentration remains nearly constant during the reaction. In this case, 'B' is in excess, but the order with respect to 'A' is still 2nd order, not pseudo order.
This option is not applicable because the rate law shows an exponent of 2 for '[A]' (Rate = k[A]2[B]). In a 1st order reaction, the exponent would be 1 with respect to '[A]'.
This option is not applicable because the rate law indicates an exponent of 2 for '[A]' and not 3.

questions.explanation: Experimental rate equation is called rate law. According to rate law equation, order is 2nd with respect to "A" while order is zero with respect to "B". Order of reaction is zero with respect to excess reactant. So, overall order of reaction is 2nd.

questions.text: Rate of first order reaction depends on:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In a first-order reaction, the rate is directly proportional to the concentration of a single reactant. This is described by the rate law: Rate = k[A], where [A] is the concentration of the reactant 'A.' Thus, this option correctly describes the dependency of the reaction rate.
This option incorrectly suggests that the rate of a first-order reaction depends on two reactants. In reality, a first-order reaction only depends on the concentration of one reactant, not two.
This option is also incorrect. A first-order reaction is defined by its dependence on the concentration of just one reactant, making this option invalid.

This option is misleading as it implies that the reaction rate does not depend on the initial concentration. However, in a first-order reaction, the rate is indeed dependent on the concentration of the reactant, which changes over the course of the reaction.

questions.explanation: In a first-order reaction, the rate depends on the concentration of one reactant. This means that the rate of the reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of that particular reactant. The rate law for a first-order reaction is typically written as Rate = k[A], where [A] represents the concentration of the reactant 'A.' So, this option is correct.

questions.text: The unit of rate constant is same as that of rate of reaction in:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In a first-order reaction, the rate law is expressed as Rate = k[A]. The unit of the rate constant (k) is time-1, while the unit of the rate of reaction is concentration unit per time, such as mol dm-3 s-1. Therefore, the units are not the same.

In a zero-order reaction, the rate law is Rate = k. The rate constant (k) has the same unit as the rate of reaction, which is usually concentration unit per time, such as mol dm-3 s-1. Thus, the units match.

For a second-order reaction, the rate law might be Rate = k[A]2. The unit of the rate constant (k) is typically (concentration unit)-1 * time-1, whereas the rate of reaction is concentration unit per time. Therefore, the units do not match.

In a third-order reaction, the rate law can be expressed as Rate = k[A]3. The unit of the rate constant (k) is (concentration unit)-2 * time-1. The rate of reaction has a unit of concentration unit per time. Clearly, the units are not the same.

questions.explanation: Rate Law: Rate = k The unit of the rate constant (k) is typically expressed as concentration unit per time. The unit of the rate of reaction (Rate) is also in concentration unit per time. Therefore, in a zero-order reaction, the unit of the rate constant is the same as the unit of the rate of reaction.

questions.text: For a chemical reaction to occur:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In many chemical reactions, reactants are placed in a vessel or container.An "open" vessel means that the reactants are not enclosed or sealed in any way, allowing for interactions with the surrounding environment.

options.text: Reacting molecules should have less energy than Ea at time of collision
options.explanation: Ea stands for activation energy, which is the minimum energy required for a chemical reaction to occur.For a reaction to start, the reacting molecules must possess kinetic energy equal to or greater than the activation energy (Ea) to overcome the energy barrier and initiate the reaction.

options.text: Reacting molecules must be properly oriented and energy more than or equal to Ea
options.explanation: In addition to having sufficient energy, reacting molecules must collide with the correct orientation.Proper orientation ensures that the reactant molecules come into contact in a way that allows the necessary chemical bonds to break and new ones to form, leading to the desired reaction.

options.text: The reacting molecules must not collide with each other
options.explanation: This statement emphasizes that reactant molecules should not collide in a way that hinders the reaction.Unproductive collisions, where molecules simply bounce off each other, do not contribute to the reaction.

questions.explanation: In addition to having sufficient energy, reacting molecules must collide with the correct orientation.Proper orientation ensures that the reactant molecules come into contact in a way that allows the necessary chemical bonds to break and new ones to form, leading to the desired reaction.

questions.text: The 2nd order reaction becomes 1st order when:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: One of the reactants is limiting
options.explanation: If concentration of a reactant remains constant (because it is a catalyst or because it is in excess with respect to other reactants), its concentration can be included in rate constant thus making(causing) the reaction to be pseudo first order or sometimes pseudo second order reaction.

options.text: One of the reactants is in large excess
options.explanation: If concentration of a reactant remains constant (because it is a catalyst or because it is in excess with respect to other reactants), its concentration can be included in rate constant thus making(causing) the reaction to be pseudo first order or sometimes pseudo second order reaction.

options.text: None of the reactants is in large excess
options.explanation: If B is in excess, it becomes pseudo second order. It is generally accepted that third order reactions are hard to occur.

The substance taken in small amount controls the rate and the order is noted with respect to that substance. If the cone. Of any reactant is taken in excess it will not involve in rate determination step.

questions.explanation: The substance taken in small amount controls the rate and the order is noted with respect to that substance. If the cone. Of any reactant is taken in excess it will not involve in rate determination step.

questions.text: For a reaction A+2B ----> C, rate is given by Rate = k[A][B], hence the order of the reaction is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The order of the reaction is determined by the sum of the exponents in the rate equation. Here, the sum is 2, not 3, making this option incorrect.

The order of the reaction is determined by the sum of the exponents of the concentration terms in the rate equation. In this case, Rate = k[A][B], with exponents 1 for [A] and 1 for [B]. The sum of these exponents is 1 + 1 = 2, indicating the reaction is second order overall.
The sum of the exponents in the rate equation is 2, not 1, making this option incorrect.
The rate equation indicates a non-zero order, specifically 2, thus making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The rate of the reaction is given by Rate = k[A][B]. The order of the reaction is found by adding the exponents of the concentration terms in the rate equation. In this equation, the exponent for [A] is 1, and the exponent for [B] is 1, giving a total order of 1 + 1 = 2. This means the reaction is second order overall.Option A (3) is incorrect because it overestimates the order. Option C (1) is incorrect because it underestimates the order. Option D (0) is incorrect because it implies no dependence on reactant concentrations, which contradicts the given rate equation.

questions.text: What will be the order of reaction if doubling the concentration of reactant increases the rate by a factor of 4 and tripling the concentration of reactant by a factor of 9?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: First-order reactions result in a linear relationship between rate and concentration, which does not match the given rate changes.

Second-order reactions imply the rate is proportional to the square of the concentration. Doubling the concentration increases the rate by a factor of 4, and tripling the concentration increases it by a factor of 9, which matches the given data.
Third-order reactions would result in the rate increasing by a factor of 8 when concentration is doubled, which is inconsistent with the given information.
A zero-order reaction would mean the rate is independent of concentration, contradicting the observation that changes in concentration affect the rate.

questions.explanation: The order of a reaction indicates how the rate depends on reactant concentration.If doubling the concentration increases the rate fourfold, and tripling increases it ninefold, the rate depends on the square of the concentration.Hence, the reaction is second order.

questions.text: The rate of reaction 2X + Y-----> ProductsRate= k[X]2 [Y] if X in large excess, the order of reaction is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Even if X is in large excess, the order with respect to X remains 2. However, since X is in large excess, its concentration change is negligible, making the overall reaction order dependent on Y only, which is not 3.

If X were not in excess, the reaction would be 2nd order with respect to X and 1st with respect to Y. However, in large excess, X's concentration effectively becomes constant, reducing the reaction order to focus on Y.
With X in large excess, the reaction rate depends primarily on Y, making it 1st order with respect to Y.
A 0th order reaction is independent of the concentration of reactants. In this case, the reaction rate is dependent on Y, so the order isn't 0.

questions.explanation: In the given reaction, 2X + Y → Products, the rate law is Rate = k[X]2[Y]. When X is in large excess, its concentration effectively remains constant throughout the reaction, making the rate independent of changes in X's concentration. Therefore, the reaction order primarily depends on Y, which is 1st order. The correct answer is 1st order because the rate is proportional to [Y] only when X is in excess. The other options incorrectly assume that the order is influenced by X, which is incorrect due to its large excess rendering its concentration constant.

questions.text: The half-life period of zero order reaction is equal to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This formula is applicable for first-order reactions, where the half-life is independent of the initial concentration.

This is the correct formula for a zero order reaction, where the half-life is directly proportional to the initial concentration of the reactant (a) and inversely proportional to the rate constant (K).
This formula does not correspond to any common order reaction. It is incorrect for zero order reactions.
This formula is incorrect and does not represent the half-life of zero, first, or second order reactions.

questions.explanation: For a zero order reaction, the half-life is given by the formula t1/2 = a/2K, where a is the initial concentration of the reactant and K is the rate constant. This means that the half-life is directly proportional to the initial concentration of the reactant. Option B is correct. Option A is the formula for the half-life of a first-order reaction, which is not applicable here. Option C and D provide incorrect formulas that do not apply to zero order reactions.

questions.text: For which of the reaction, half-life is inversely proportional to the initial concentration of the reactants?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: For a zero-order reaction, the half-life is given by t1/2 = [A]0/2k, making it directly proportional to the initial concentration of the reactants.
The half-life of a first-order reaction is t1/2 = 0.693/k, which is independent of the initial concentration.
For a second-order reaction, the half-life is t1/2 = 1/(k[A]0), meaning it is inversely proportional to the initial concentration of the reactants.
While more complex, the half-life for third-order reactions is not inversely proportional to the initial concentration in a straightforward manner like second-order reactions.

questions.explanation: In a second-order reaction, the half-life formula t1/2 = 1/(k[A]0) shows that the half-life is inversely proportional to the initial concentration of the reactants. This means that as the initial concentration increases, the half-life decreases. For a zero-order reaction, the half-life is directly proportional to the initial concentration. In a first-order reaction, the half-life is independent of initial concentration, while third-order reactions have a more complex relationship that is not simply inversely proportional.

questions.text: For the first order reaction, half-life is 14 sec. The time requires for the initial concentration to reduce to 1/8th of its value is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect. It takes 3 half-lives, not 2, to reduce the concentration to 1/8th. Each half-life is 14 seconds, so the total time is 42 seconds.
Correct. For a first-order reaction, reducing the concentration to 1/8th requires 3 half-lives. Since each half-life is 14 seconds, 3 x 14 = 42 seconds.
Incorrect. The time is not squared; it should be the number of half-lives multiplied by the half-life duration: 3 x 14 = 42 seconds.
Incorrect. The time is not cubed; it should be the number of half-lives multiplied by the half-life duration: 3 x 14 = 42 seconds.

questions.explanation: For a first-order reaction, the half-life is the time it takes for the concentration to reduce to half its initial value. Given that the half-life is 14 seconds, we need to determine how many half-lives it takes to reduce the concentration to 1/8th of its initial value. Since (1/2)3 = 1/8, it takes 3 half-lives to reach this concentration. Therefore, the total time required is 3 x 14 = 42 seconds. Option B is correct. Option A incorrectly assumes only 2 half-lives are needed. Options C and D incorrectly manipulate the half-life duration.

questions.text: If Ef and Er are activation energies of forward and reverse reaction. The reaction is known to be exothermic then:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option suggests that the ativation energy for the forward reaction is more. But when the forward reaction is exothermic, net energy is released rather than absorbed.
This option suggests that the ativation energy for the forward reaction is more. But when the forward reaction is exothermic, net energy is released rather than absorbed.

An exothermic reaction takes place when energy is released into the surroundings, by the forward reaction. So heat energy required by the forward reaction is far less than that of reverse reaction. Rather than absorbing energy, it releases it. Therefore, the activation energy for the forward reaction is less.
Activation energy for the forward reaction is less since it releases energy. So, they are not equal.

questions.explanation: The explanation is given in image.

questions.text: The change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time is called rate of reaction. The rate of reaction:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The rate of reaction increases as the reaction proceeds
options.explanation: Incorrect. As the reaction proceeds, the concentration of reactants decreases, which typically leads to a decrease in the rate of reaction. An increase in the rate would generally require external factors like increased temperature or catalysts.

options.text: The rate of reaction decreases as the reaction proceeds
options.explanation: Correct. As the reaction progresses, the concentration of reactants typically decreases while products accumulate, leading to fewer collisions and a slower rate of reaction.

options.text: The rate of reaction remains the same as the reaction proceeds
options.explanation: Incorrect. The rate of reaction is influenced by the concentration of reactants, which generally decreases over time, leading to a slower rate.

options.text: The rate of reaction may decrease or increase as the reaction proceeds
options.explanation: Incorrect. While external factors can alter the rate, under normal conditions without intervention, the rate typically decreases as reactants are consumed.

questions.explanation: The rate of reaction typically decreases as the reaction progresses due to the depletion of reactants and the accumulation of products. This results in fewer effective collisions between reactant molecules, thus slowing the reaction rate. Option A is incorrect because the rate usually does not increase without external influences. Option C is incorrect because the rate of reaction does not remain constant due to changing concentrations. Option D is misleading without additional context, as natural progression generally leads to a decrease in rate.

questions.text: All of the following factors affect rate of reaction EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect. The concentration of reactants is a key factor affecting the rate of reaction. Higher concentrations lead to more frequent collisions, accelerating the reaction rate.

Correct. Molecularity describes the number of molecules involved in an elementary reaction step. It is a concept used to understand reaction mechanisms, but it does not directly affect the overall reaction rate.
Incorrect. Catalysts are substances that increase the reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy, thereby speeding up the reaction without being consumed.

Incorrect. Temperature is a critical factor that affects reaction rates. Increasing temperature boosts molecular kinetic energy, leading to more frequent and energetic collisions, thus increasing the reaction rate.

questions.explanation: Molecularity is the correct answer as it refers to the number of reacting species in an elementary step of a mechanism, but it does not directly influence the overall rate of a chemical reaction. The rate of reaction is predominantly influenced by factors such as the concentration of reactants, which determines collision frequency; catalysts, which lower activation energy; and temperature, which increases kinetic energy and collision effectiveness. Therefore, while molecularity is essential for understanding reaction mechanisms, it doesn’t directly impact the rate at which reactions occur.

questions.text: The addition of catalyst to a reaction can.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Enthalpy refers to the total heat content of a system. Adding a catalyst to a reaction typically does not directly change the enthalpy of the reaction. Catalysts provide an alternate reaction pathway with lower activation energy, but they don't change the energy difference between reactants and products (enthalpy change). So, this option is incorrect.

Entropy is a measure of the disorder or randomness in a system. Adding a catalyst usually does not directly impact the entropy of the reactants, products, or the reaction itself. Therefore, this option is generally incorrect.

While catalysts can indirectly affect the nature of products by influencing reaction pathways, they don't change the underlying energy differences that determine which products are favored. So, the more accurate statement is that catalysts primarily impact the rate of the reaction by changing the activation energy, not the nature of products. Incorrect.

This is the most accurate statement. A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. By lowering the activation energy, a catalyst speeds up the rate of the reaction without itself being consumed. This allows the reaction to occur more readily under milder conditions. So, this option is Correct.

questions.explanation: Option D is the most accurate statement. A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. By lowering the activation energy, a catalyst speeds up the rate of the reaction without itself being consumed. This allows the reaction to occur more readily under milder conditions.

questions.text: If rate of reaction decay of a radioactive isotope decreases from 200 counts per minutes to 25 counts per minute after 24 hours. What is half life?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is incorrect. A half-life of 3 hours would result in a faster decay rate than observed over 24 hours.
This is incorrect. A half-life of 6 hours would result in more than 3 half-lives in 24 hours, which would reduce the count to less than 25 counts per minute.
This is incorrect. A half-life of 4 hours would lead to a decay through 6 half-lives in 24 hours, reducing the count far below 25 counts per minute.
This is correct. A half-life of 8 hours means the isotope undergoes 3 half-lives in 24 hours, reducing the count from 200 to 25 counts per minute.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct. The half-life of 8 hours is determined by the observation that the decay rate reduces from 200 counts per minute to 25 counts per minute over 24 hours. This involves 3 half-lives, as each half-life reduces the number of radioactive atoms to half its previous value: 200 → 100 → 50 → 25 counts per minute. Thus, a half-life of 8 hours results in the observed decay pattern. Other options suggest shorter half-lives, which would cause the decay rate to drop to below 25 counts per minute within 24 hours, which is inconsistent with the given data.

questions.text: All of the following are characteristic features of catalyst EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It speeds up a chemical reaction
options.explanation: Speeds up a chemical reaction. A catalyst lowers the activation energy of a reaction, which allows the reaction to occur more quickly.

options.text: It is used in smaller amount
options.explanation: Is used in small amounts. A catalyst is used in small amounts because it is not consumed in the reaction.

options.text: Does not change the equilibrium constant of a reaction
options.explanation: Does not change the equilibrium constant of a reaction. A catalyst does not change the equilibrium constant of a reaction. The equilibrium constant is a measure of the relative concentrations of the reactants and products at equilibrium.
Correct option.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct.1.Speeds up a chemical reaction. A catalyst lowers the activation energy of a reaction, which allows the reaction to occur more quickly. 2.Used in small amounts. A catalyst is used in small amounts because it is not consumed in the reaction. 3.Does not change the equilibrium constant of a reaction. A catalyst does not change the equilibrium constant of a reaction. The equilibrium constant is a measure of the relative concentrations of the reactants and products at equilibrium.

questions.text: dx/dt is rate of change of concentration with respect to
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The expression 'dx/dt' refers to the rate of change of a variable with respect to time, not temperature. If it were related to temperature, it would be expressed as 'dx/dT'. Thus, this option is incorrect.
The expression 'dx/dt' is about the change over time, not pressure. If pressure were the variable of interest, the expression would be 'dx/dP'. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
The expression 'dx/dt' directly indicates the rate of change of a variable x with respect to time t. Hence, this is the correct option.
This option is incorrect because 'dx/dt' clearly refers to the rate of change with respect to time, making 'Time' the correct choice.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Time' because 'dx/dt' is a standard notation for representing the rate of change of a variable x over time t. This is fundamental in reaction kinetics where the concentration of a reactant or product changes over time. The other options, 'Temperature' and 'Pressure', involve different derivatives ('dx/dT' and 'dx/dP', respectively) and are thus incorrect in this context. 'None of these' is also incorrect as 'Time' is indeed the correct match for the given expression.

questions.text: Drawing a graph between concentration change with time gives a:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect. A straight line implies a constant rate of change, which is not typical for most reaction kinetics involving concentration changes over time.
Incorrect. A parabolic shape is not typical for concentration vs. time graphs in reaction kinetics, as it suggests a specific, uncommon relationship between concentration and time.
Correct. In reaction kinetics, a graph of concentration vs. time is usually a curve, reflecting how the rate of reaction changes over time.
Incorrect. A scattered graph does not accurately represent the systematic change in concentration over time that is expected in reaction kinetics.

questions.explanation: Drawing a graph between concentration change with time typically gives a representation of the rate of a chemical reaction. Such a graph is commonly known as a "rate-time" or "concentration-time" graph.In a rate-time graph, the concentration of a reactant or product is plotted on the y-axis (vertical axis), while time is plotted on the x-axis (horizontal axis). The graph shows how the concentration of the substance changes over time during the course of the reaction.The slope or steepness of the graph at a particular point represents the rate of the reaction at that time. A steeper slope indicates a higher rate of reaction, while a shallower slope suggests a slower rate. The shape of the graph can vary depending on the order of the reaction and the specific reaction kinetics.Typically, in a reaction involving the disappearance of a reactant, the concentration decreases over time, resulting in a downward-sloping curve. Conversely, in a reaction involving the formation of a product, the concentration increases over time, resulting in an upward-sloping curve.The rate-time graph provides valuable information about the reaction kinetics, including the initial rate, reaction order, and potential rate-determining steps. It allows for the quantitative analysis of the reaction rate and provides insights into the reaction mechanism and behavior.

questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect option.N represents order of reactions.
Incorrect option.N represents order of reactions.
Incorrect option.N represents order of reactions.
Correct option.N represents order of reactions.

questions.explanation: N represents order of reactions.

questions.text: Rate constant is denoted by?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Correct option. The rate constant in chemical reactions is universally denoted by the symbol "k". It signifies the constant of proportionality that ties the rate of reaction to the concentrations of the reactants involved.
Incorrect option. The symbol "kv" is not a standard representation of the rate constant in the context of chemical kinetics.
Incorrect option. The symbol "kf" may be used to denote the rate constant for a forward reaction in certain contexts but is not the general symbol for the rate constant.
Incorrect option. The symbol "ks" is not a recognized notation for the rate constant in standard chemical kinetics.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is "k" as it is the standard symbol used to represent the rate constant in chemical kinetics. The rate constant is a crucial parameter that connects the rate of a chemical reaction to the concentrations of the reactants. It provides insights into the speed and dynamics of the reaction under specific conditions. The other options, such as "kv", "kf", and "ks", are not standard symbols for the rate constant, although "kf" might sometimes be used to specifically denote a forward reaction rate constant, it is not the general symbol.

questions.text: As the time interval approaches zero the average and instantaneous rate become ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The rates of reaction becoming negative is unrelated to the concept of time interval approaching zero. The rates of reaction are not inherently negative as time intervals decrease.
The rates of reaction do not become zero as the time interval approaches zero. Instead, the average rate over a small time interval converges to the instantaneous rate.

As the time interval approaches zero, the average rate and the instantaneous rate of a reaction become equal. This is because the average rate over a very small time interval approximates the instantaneous rate at that moment in time.
The positivity of rates is not affected by the time interval approaching zero. This option does not address the convergence of average and instantaneous rates.

questions.explanation: When considering reaction kinetics, the average rate of reaction over a given time interval is calculated as the change in concentration divided by the change in time. As the time interval becomes infinitesimally small, this average rate approaches the instantaneous rate, which is the rate at a specific point in time. Thus, the average and instantaneous rates become equal as the interval approaches zero. This is why Option C is correct. Options A and D are incorrect as they do not relate to the concept of average and instantaneous rates becoming equal. Option B is incorrect because the rates do not become zero; they converge to be equal.

questions.text: How much percentage of reactants is converted into products during half-life period?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 40% is not the correct percentage converted during a half-life. In a typical reaction, half of the reactants are converted into products after one half-life, which is 50%.
70% is too high. During one half-life, only 50% of the reactants are converted into products in any order of reaction.
This is the correct option. By definition, a half-life is the time required for half of the reactants to be converted into products.
30% is too low. The correct conversion during a half-life is 50%, meaning that half of the reactants are converted into products.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 50%. By definition, the half-life of a reaction is the time required for the concentration of a reactant to decrease to half of its initial value. This means that, regardless of the reaction order, 50% of the reactants are converted into products during one half-life. Options A, B, and D (40%, 70%, and 30% respectively) are incorrect because they do not match the definition of half-life, which is consistently 50% conversion.

questions.text: The activation energy for forward and reverse reaction can be lowered by?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Lowering the temperature typically reduces the kinetic energy of the molecules, which can slow down a reaction rather than lower its activation energy. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
Decreasing pressure affects the concentration of gases involved in a reaction but does not alter the activation energy required for the reaction. Thus, this option is incorrect.

A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy for both the forward and reverse reactions. This allows the reaction to proceed more quickly without altering the equilibrium position, making this the correct option.
This option is incorrect because neither lowering the temperature nor decreasing the pressure lowers the activation energy. Only a catalyst has this effect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that a catalyst lowers the activation energy for both forward and reverse reactions by providing an alternative pathway. This accelerates the reaction without changing the equilibrium. Lowering temperature generally slows reactions and does not affect activation energy, while decreasing pressure impacts the rate by changing concentrations but not the activation energy. Hence, only a catalyst effectively reduces the activation energy.

questions.text: The rate expression have a negative sign for
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The rate expression for the disappearance of reactants is negative because it represents the decrease in reactant concentration over time. The negative sign indicates the reactant is being consumed in the reaction.

The rate expression for the formation of products is positive, as it represents the increase in product concentration over time. There is no negative sign because the products are being generated, not consumed.

Time itself does not carry a positive or negative sign in the context of rate expressions. It is simply a variable that the rate is measured against, so this is not applicable to the question about the negative sign.

This option is incorrect because only the disappearance of reactants is associated with a negative sign in the rate expression. The formation of products has a positive sign, and time does not have a sign in this context.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the disappearance of reactants. In chemical kinetics, the rate of a reaction can be expressed as the change in concentration of reactants or products over time. For reactants, the concentration decreases as the reaction progresses, which is represented by a negative sign in the rate expression, indicating consumption. In contrast, the formation of products is accompanied by a positive change in concentration, so the rate expression is positive. Time is a neutral variable in this context and does not affect the sign of the rate expression.

questions.text: Rate of reaction has :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the rate of a chemical reaction is always expressed in terms of concentration change per unit time, which means it has units.
This is not a complete representation of the units for rate of reaction. It lacks the time component, which is essential to define how quickly the concentration changes.
Similar to Option B, this option is incomplete. The rate of reaction needs to include both concentration and time to be fully defined.
This is the correct unit for expressing the rate of reaction. It indicates how the concentration of reactants or products changes over time.

questions.explanation: The rate of reaction is properly expressed in units of mol dm-3 s-1, meaning the change in concentration (moles per cubic decimeter) per second. This unit captures both the transformation of reactants to products and the time aspect of the reaction, making Option D the correct choice. Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they either omit the time component or fail to accurately represent the units required for expressing the rate of reaction.

questions.text: Order of reaction can be determined
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While theory can suggest possible orders based on mechanisms, it cannot definitively determine the order of a reaction.
The correct method to determine the order of a reaction is through experimental data, where the concentration of reactants and their effect on reaction rate are measured.

options.text: Summing up the exponents of rate equation
options.explanation: This method can be used to determine the overall order once the rate equation is known, but it requires experimental data to establish the rate equation itself.
This option is incorrect because not all listed methods are valid for determining the order of reaction. Only experimental methods provide a definitive determination.

questions.explanation: The order of a reaction is determined experimentally by measuring how the concentration of reactants affects the rate of reaction. Theoretically, one might propose a mechanism suggesting a certain order, but this must be confirmed through experiments. Summing the exponents in the rate equation is a method for calculating the overall order once the rate law is known, which again requires experimental data to establish. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Experimentally,' as it is the practical method used to determine the order of a reaction.

questions.text: Production of ammonia by Haber's process is a/an:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An endothermic reaction absorbs heat from its surroundings. Since the Haber process releases heat, it is not endothermic. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
The Haber process for ammonia production releases energy in the form of heat, making it an exothermic reaction. Therefore, this is the correct option.

An irreversible reaction proceeds only in one direction. The Haber process is a reversible reaction as it can reach equilibrium, represented by the equation: N2 + 3H2 ⇌ 2NH3 + heat. Hence, this option is incorrect.

A redox reaction involves electron transfer between reactants. The Haber process does not primarily involve electron transfer, but rather the breaking and forming of covalent bonds. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The Haber process, used for the production of ammonia, is an exothermic reaction. This means it releases energy in the form of heat, driving the reaction forward. The other options are incorrect because: An endothermic reaction absorbs heat, which contradicts the nature of the Haber process; An irreversible reaction describes a process that goes to completion in one direction, while the Haber process is reversible, allowing the reaction to reach equilibrium; A redox reaction requires electron transfer, which is not the primary mechanism in the Haber process.

questions.text: The activated complex is formed due to
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Pressure can influence the reaction rate in gas-phase reactions by changing the concentration of reactant molecules. However, it is not the direct cause of the formation of an activated complex. Thus, this option is not correct.

Temperature can increase the rate of a reaction by providing energy to the reactants, but it does not directly form the activated complex. This option is incorrect because temperature alone does not ensure the formation of an activated complex.

An activated complex is formed when reactant molecules collide with sufficient energy and proper orientation to overcome the activation energy barrier. Therefore, effective collisions are crucial for the formation of an activated complex, making this option correct.

Ineffective collisions lack the necessary energy or correct orientation to surpass the activation energy barrier, preventing the formation of an activated complex. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Effective collision. An activated complex, also known as a transition state, is formed when reactant molecules collide with enough energy and the correct orientation to overcome the activation energy barrier. This is known as an effective collision. Other factors, such as pressure and temperature, can influence the rate of reaction or the energy available to molecules, but they do not directly result in the formation of the activated complex. Ineffective collisions, on the other hand, do not have the necessary conditions to form an activated complex.

questions.text: In general photochemical reactions are of order
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Photochemical reactions often proceed at a constant rate when light intensity is constant, making them zero-order reactions.
First-order reactions depend on the concentration of one reactant, which is not typically the case for photochemical reactions under constant illumination.
Second-order reactions depend on the concentrations of two reactants or the square of the concentration of one reactant, which doesn't generally apply to photochemical reactions.
Third-order reactions are rare and depend on the concentrations of three reactants, which is not applicable to typical photochemical reactions.

questions.explanation: Photochemical reactions are driven by light and often proceed at a constant rate when the light intensity is steady, resulting in a zero-order reaction. The reaction rate is independent of the concentration of the reactants. Options B, C, and D describe reactions that depend on the concentration of reactants, which is not characteristic of photochemical reactions.

questions.text: Rusting of iron is the example of _ reaction:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Rusting of iron is not a fast process. The reaction between iron, oxygen, and water happens slowly over time, making this option incorrect.
Rusting of iron is indeed a slow process. It involves a gradual reaction of iron with oxygen and water to form iron oxide (rust), due to the low reactivity of iron under normal conditions.
The reaction rate of rusting is not moderate. Rusting occurs much more slowly than moderate or fast reactions, making this option incorrect.
While environmental conditions can influence the rate of rusting, it is fundamentally a slow process under typical atmospheric conditions. Hence, this option is not appropriate.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Slow. Rusting of iron is a slow process because it involves a gradual reaction with oxygen and water to form iron oxide, also known as rust. This occurs due to the relatively low reactivity of iron and typical atmospheric conditions. The formation of a protective layer of rust on the surface further slows down the process. Fast is incorrect as rusting does not occur quickly. Moderate is not accurate as the rate of rusting is slower than moderate reactions. Depends upon conditions is misleading because, while environmental factors can affect the rate, rusting is inherently a slow reaction.

questions.text: Choose the correct option.
questions.subject: English

options.text: Samar bought an apple, an orange and an pear.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because "an pear" should be corrected to "a pear". The article "an" is used before words that start with a vowel sound, and "pear" starts with a consonant sound, so it should be "a pear".

options.text: Samar bought an apple a orange and a pear.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it does not use commas to separate the items in the list ("an apple", "a orange", and "a pear"). Commas are needed to separate items in a series.

options.text: Samar bought an apple, an orange and a pear.
options.explanation: This option is correct. It correctly uses commas to separate the items in the list ("an apple", "an orange", and "a pear"), and the articles ("an" and "a") are used correctly.

options.text: Samar bought a apple, an orange and a pear.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because "a apple" should be corrected to "an apple". The article "a" is used before words that start with a consonant sound, and "apple" starts with a vowel sound, so it should be "an apple".

questions.explanation: This option is correct. It correctly uses commas to separate the items in the list ("an apple", "an orange", and "a pear"), and the articles ("an" and "a") are used correctly.

questions.text: John _ to be a doctor.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is the correct answer because it is grammatically correct and makes sense in the context of the sentence. "John wants to be a doctor" indicates his desire or aspiration.
Incorrect form of verb used according to tense.Wanting: This is incorrect because it is a present participle and needs a helping verb (like "is" or "are") to form a complete sentence.

Incorrect form of verb used according to tense.Is wanting: While grammatically correct, this option suggests a continuous action, implying John is in the process of wanting to be a doctor, which doesn't fit the context as well as a simple statement of desire.

Incorrect form of verb used according to tense.Have wanted: This option implies that John's desire to be a doctor started in the past and continues to the present. While possible, "wants" is a more straightforward and common way to express a current desire.

questions.explanation: “Wants” is the correct answer because it is the simple present tense and correctly expresses John’s current desire. The sentence ‘John wants to be a doctor’ is grammatically correct and makes complete sense in context.”

This part of the sentence is correct. It specifies that the chapters being referred to are from the book mentioned.
This part of the sentence is correct. It describes the level of interest the speaker had in the two chapters.

questions.text: We went to the President as a last _.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Recourse" is a similar word that means seeking help or protection from someone or something when in difficulty or trouble, but it doesn't fit well in the given sentence.
A last resort is a final course of action, used only when all else has failed.
"Resource" refers to a source of aid or support that can be drawn upon when needed. While it may be used in the same context as "last resort", it does not fit the given sentence.
"Resound" means to echo or reverberate with sound and has no connection to the given sentence.

questions.text: The company _ of its stakeholders.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is the base form of the verb "think," which is used with plural subjects (e.g., they, we, companies). However, it doesn't fit grammatically in the given sentence.
"Thought" is the past tense of the verb "think." It doesn't fit the sentence because the sentence requires a present tense verb.
"Thinks" is the third-person singular form of the verb "think." It matches with the subject "the company," which is singular, making it grammatically correct in the sentence.

"Thinking" is the present participle form of the verb "think." It is used to form continuous tenses (e.g., the company is thinking, the company was thinking), but it doesn't fit the given sentence as it doesn't express a complete action.

questions.explanation: In the given sentence, "The company" is a singular subject, so it should be followed by a singular verb form. The present tense form "thinks" agrees with the singular subject "company." Therefore, the correct option is C) Thinks.

questions.text: Statement:The Management of School M has decided to give free breakfast from next academic year to all the students in its primary section through its canteen even though they will not get any government grant.Courses of Action:I. The school will have to admit many poor students who will seek admission for the next academic year.II. The canteen facilities and utensils have to be checked and new purchases to be made to equip it properly.III. Funds will have to be raised to support the scheme for years to come.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Let's analyze each course of action in response to the given statement:Statement: The Management of School M has decided to give free breakfast from next academic year to all the students in its primary section through its canteen even though they will not get any government grant.Courses of Action:I. The school will have to admit many poor students who will seek admission for the next academic year.II. The canteen facilities and utensils have to be checked, and new purchases to be made to equip it properly.III. Funds will have to be raised to support the scheme for years to come.Let's evaluate each course of action:I. The school will have to admit many poor students who will seek admission for the next academic year.This course of action suggests that offering free breakfast to all students in the primary section may attract many poor students to seek admission at the school. Providing free breakfast could be a positive incentive for economically disadvantaged families to consider enrolling their children in the school.II. The canteen facilities and utensils have to be checked, and new purchases to be made to equip it properly.This course of action suggests that the canteen facilities and utensils need to be assessed and upgraded to ensure they can cater to the increased demand for providing free breakfast to all primary students. Proper equipment and facilities are essential for efficiently running the breakfast program.III. Funds will have to be raised to support the scheme for years to come.This course of action highlights the need for raising funds to sustain the free breakfast program in the long term. Offering free breakfast to all primary students will involve ongoing costs for procuring food items and maintaining the canteen, especially if the school is not receiving any government grant for the initiative.To summarize, the courses of action outline some steps that the school management needs to consider after deciding to provide free breakfast to all primary students. These steps include potentially admitting more students, checking and upgrading the canteen facilities, and raising funds to sustain the program. The success of the initiative will depend on effective planning and resource management by the school management.
Since the school has introduced the scheme without receiving any grant, it needs to pool up the funds and make adequate arrangements to keep the scheme going. So, only II and III follow.
Since the school has introduced the scheme without receiving any grant, it needs to pool up the funds and make adequate arrangements to keep the scheme going. So, only II and III follow.
Since the school has introduced the scheme without receiving any grant, it needs to pool up the funds and make adequate arrangements to keep the scheme going. So, only II and III follow.

questions.explanation: Since the school has introduced the scheme without receiving any grant, it needs to pool up the funds and make adequate arrangements to keep the scheme going. So, only II and III follow.
